ranked_chunks.title: RazaAli313/Automating-Excel-WorkFlows-Using-Python:README.md
ranked_chunks.text: Excel Workflow Automation with OpenPyXL This Python script automates Excel spreadsheet processing using the openpyxl library. It modifies numerical values, adds a bar chart, and saves an updated version of the file. Features Reads an Excel file and processes data in Sheet1. Updates values in the third column (reducing by 10%) and writes them to the fourth column. Adds a bar chart to visualize the updated values. Saves a new Excel file with modifications. Requirements Python 3.x openpyxl library Installation bash Copy Edit pip install openpyxl Usage Ensure your Excel file (e.g., sales.xlsx) exists in the script's directory. Run the script with: python Copy Edit process_spreadsheet("sales.xlsx") The modified file is saved as sales_updated.xlsx.

Python – Data Cleaning & EDA The raw dataset contained inconsistent data types, missing values, and noisy text fields. ✔ Cleaning Steps: Converted Age & Spending to numeric Converted dates to datetime Handled missing values using median and default categories Normalized text fields (Gender / Purpose / Feedback) Removed corrupt or invalid rows Created new fields: Duration MonthNumber ✔ EDA Highlights: Age distribution ≈ 44 years No correlation between Age and Spending Spending range: 300–4998 USD Dataset fully cleaned for further analysis 🔗 Notebook: python_analysis.ipynb 📊 2. Excel – Business Analysis (10 Questions) Using Pivot Tables, I analyzed: Top countries → Egypt & Germany (1491 visitors each) Most common purpose → Business & Leisure Highest spending country → Saint Martin Gender distribution → Male 7311 | Female 7112 Highest spending age group → 50+ Peak month → May (1320 visitors) Average duration → 11.54 days Most common feedback → OK 🔗 Excel File: clean_tourism_data.xlsx 📈 3.

ranked_chunks.title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:README.md
ranked_chunks.text: ![MCP Excel Server](imgs/mcp_excel_server.jpg) **MCP Excel Server** is an implementation of the MCP Server that provides tools for automating Excel file operations using Python and [openpyxl](https://openpyxl.readthedocs.io/). It allows you to: - Create, open, and save Excel workbooks - Add, rename, copy, move, and delete worksheets - Read and write data to cells and ranges - Merge, unmerge, copy, move, and delete cell ranges - List available Excel files ---

ranked_chunks.title: rogers-cyber/SmartExcelGuardian:README.md
ranked_chunks.section_title: SmartExcelGuardian v1.1.0 – Professional Excel Data Guardian Tool (Full Source Code)
ranked_chunks.text: SmartExcelGuardian v1.1.0 is a powerful Python desktop application for **professional Excel data cleanup, validation, and monitoring**. This repository contains the **full source code**, allowing you to customize heuristic scoring, formula cleanup, conditional formatting, export logic, and UI behavior for enterprise reporting, analytics, or auditing workflows. ------------------------------------------------------------ 🌟 SCREENSHOT ------------------------------------------------------------ ------------------------------------------------------------ 🌟 FEATURES ------------------------------------------------------------ - 📂 Excel File Input — Load `.xlsx` or `.xls` workbooks - 🧹 Automatic Data Cleanup — Handles missing values, duplicates, and type inconsistencies - 🧠 Heuristic Scoring Engine (0–100) — Flags high-risk columns based on data quality - 📝 Column Name Normalization — snake_case rename suggestions - 🔢 Type Normalization — Numeric and string coercion with validation - 📊 Missing Value Imputation — Mean (numeric) and Mode (string) - 📈 Duplicate

ranked_chunks.title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/fastapi_reference/README.md
ranked_chunks.text: Accepts an uploaded workbook and runs the import workflow. Request: - multipart form-data - file field name: `file` - optional form field: `tenant_id` Example: Example JSON response: If the workbook has validation errors, the same endpoint still returns a structured result payload. Application code can then read: - workbook-level result status from `result` - created row count from `created_rows` - uploaded result workbook names from `uploaded_artifacts` - cell-level frontend payloads from `cell_errors` - row-level frontend payloads from `row_errors` In the reference app, those values live under `data`, while `ok` tells the client whether the route returned a success or an API-layer error envelope. Example validation-error response shape: Example API-layer error response: In a real project, you would typically extend this response with: - request-scoped trace metadata - actor information - domain-specific identifiers or import job ids - your own error-code to UI-action mapping

ranked_chunks.title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/SKILL.md
ranked_chunks.text: Once all sections are reviewed and locked, generate the final document. Read `references/docx-generator.md` for the complete template code and formatting specifications. Key formatting requirements: - Single page for anyone under 10 years of experience (hard constraint) - Two pages maximum for 10+ years - All dates and locations right-aligned using tab stops - Key metrics bolded in experience bullets - Product names, company names, and links clickable (ExternalHyperlink) - Section headings with bottom border separator - Arial font throughout - Proper bullet points using LevelFormat.BULLET (never unicode bullets) - Output both .docx and .pdf After generating, preview the output and verify: - Fits on target page count - All links are clickable - Bold formatting renders correctly - Right-alignment is clean - No orphaned lines on a second page
